questions.to_find: What mathematical value does the algorithm compute/approximate?

understand.plain: The algorithm starts with x = m and y = 1, then repeatedly updates x and y using the formulas x = (x+y)/2 and y = m/x until x and y are close enough (differ by less than epsilon). The final value of x is printed. This is a well-known iterative method for computing the square root of m.

keywords.explain: An iterative technique where each iteration refines an approximation by averaging a guess with m divided by that guess, converging to sqrt(m).
keywords.example: For m=9: x=9,y=1 -> x=5,y=1.8 -> x=3.4,y=2.65 -> converges to 3 = sqrt(9)

keywords.explain: The loop runs while x - y > epsilon; when x and y are nearly equal (within epsilon), the loop stops and x approximates sqrt(m).
keywords.example: If epsilon=0.01, the loop stops when |x - y| <= 0.01

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='380' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Babylonian Square Root</text><text x='200' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>x = (x+y)/2,  y = m/x</text><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Iteration: x and y converge to sqrt(m)</text><text x='200' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Invariant: x * y = m at each step</text><text x='200' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>When x = y  =>  x^2 = m  =>  x = sqrt(m)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Illustration of the Babylonian method: x and y bracket sqrt(m) and converge together; when x equals y, x squared equals m, so x is sqrt(m).

given.aim: Identify what mathematical value the given iterative algorithm computes.

terms.meaning: Starting guesses that bracket sqrt(m) since sqrt(m) lies between 1 and m (for m>1).
terms.example: m=16: x=16, y=1, sqrt(16)=4 lies between them
terms.connects: The algorithm keeps x >= sqrt(m) >= y, narrowing the gap each iteration.

terms.meaning: Arithmetic mean of current x and y, which updates the upper estimate.
terms.connects: Combined with y=m/x, this is exactly the Newton-Raphson update for f(x)=x^2-m.

terms.meaning: If x is an overestimate of sqrt(m), then m/x is an underestimate; their average is closer to sqrt(m).
terms.connects: Ensures the product x*y = m is maintained, so convergence target is sqrt(m).

given.plan: Recognize the update rule x_new = (x + m/x)/2 as the classic Newton-Raphson iteration for finding sqrt(m) (the positive root of f(x) = x^2 - m = 0). The loop terminates when x and y are within epsilon of each other, meaning x has converged to sqrt(m).

steps.title: Substitute y = m/x into the update rule
steps.apply: In each iteration: y = m/x, then x = (x + m/x)/2. This is precisely the Newton-Raphson update for solving x^2 = m, i.e., finding sqrt(m).
steps.note: Newton-Raphson for f(x) = x^2 - m gives x_new = x - f(x)/f'(x) = x - (x^2-m)/(2x) = (x + m/x)/2.

steps.title: Analyze convergence condition
steps.formula_raw: x - y > epsilon => x - m/x > epsilon
steps.apply: The loop stops when x - y <= epsilon, i.e., x and y are nearly equal. At convergence x = y => x = m/x => x^2 = m => x = sqrt(m). So x approximates sqrt(m).
steps.note: The algorithm is guaranteed to converge for m>1 because the arithmetic mean is always >= geometric mean, and both bracket sqrt(m).

solution.result: The algorithm approximates m^(1/2) = sqrt(m). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Newton-Raphson iteration for square root: x_(n+1) = (x_n + m/x_n)/2, which converges to sqrt(m) (Babylonian method).

questions.to_find: What output does the program print?

understand.plain: The function foo() recursively extracts digits of n from the least significant side (using n%10), recurses on the remaining digits, then prints each digit after the recursive call returns. main() calls foo(2048, 0) and then prints sum (which is 0 because sum is passed by value and not modified in main). The recursion prints digits in order from least significant to most significant... wait: the printf is after the recursive call, so digits are printed after the deepest call returns — meaning the first digit printed is the one from the deepest recursion level (most significant digit).

keywords.explain: In C, int parameters are copied; changes inside foo do not affect the caller's variable. So sum in main() remains 0.
keywords.example: foo(a, sum) copies sum=0; foo modifies its local copy but main's sum stays 0.

keywords.explain: printf is called AFTER the recursive call foo(j,sum), so deeper recursion levels print first; the digit of the most significant part is printed first when unwinding.
keywords.example: foo(2048): recurses all the way down, then prints 2, then 0, then 4, then 8 as it unwinds.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='410' height='230' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Call Stack Trace for foo(2048,0)</text><text x='30' y='60' class='sub ink'>foo(2048,0): k=8, j=204 -> recurse, print 8</text><text x='30' y='85' class='sub ink'>  foo(204,8):  k=4, j=20  -> recurse, print 4</text><text x='30' y='110' class='sub ink'>    foo(20,12): k=0, j=2   -> recurse, print 0</text><text x='30' y='135' class='sub ink'>      foo(2,12):  k=2, j=0   -> recurse, print 2</text><text x='30' y='160' class='sub ink'>        foo(0,14):  n==0 -> return</text><text x='30' y='190' class='sub ink'>Unwind order prints: 2, 0, 4, 8</text><text x='30' y='215' class='sub ink'>main prints sum=0 (unchanged, pass-by-value)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call stack showing foo(2048) -> foo(204) -> foo(20) -> foo(2) -> foo(0); on unwind, digits 2,0,4,8 are printed; main prints sum=0.

given.aim: Trace the recursive function to determine the exact output printed.

terms.meaning: Extract the last digit (k) and the remaining number (j) at each recursive step.
terms.connects: Drives the recursion by peeling off digits one at a time.

terms.meaning: The recursive call happens first, so all deeper calls complete before the current level's printf runs.
terms.example: foo(2048) waits for foo(204) which waits for ... foo(0) returns, then prints happen in reverse digit order.
terms.connects: Results in printing digits from most significant to least significant (2,0,4,8).

terms.meaning: sum in main is not modified by foo; main's printf("%d", sum) prints the original value 0.
terms.example: Even though foo accumulates sum internally (reaching 14), main's sum stays 0.
terms.connects: The last number printed is 0, eliminating options A and C.

given.plan: Trace the recursion: extract digits of 2048 from right (8,4,0,2), build call stack, then unwind printing digits in reverse extraction order (2,0,4,8). Finally main prints sum=0 (unchanged by value semantics).

steps.title: Trace the recursive calls (descent phase)
steps.apply:
foo(2048,0): k=8, j=204, sum_local=8, calls foo(204,8)
foo(204,8): k=4, j=20, sum_local=12, calls foo(20,12)
foo(20,12): k=0, j=2, sum_local=12, calls foo(2,12)
foo(2,12): k=2, j=0, sum_local=14, calls foo(0,14)
foo(0,14): n==0, returns immediately
steps.note: All four active frames hold their k values (8,4,0,2) waiting for the recursive call to return before printing.

steps.title: Unwind the call stack (print phase)
steps.formula_raw: printf("%d,", k) after recursive call returns
steps.apply:
foo(2,14) returns from foo(0,14): prints k=2 -> output so far: '2,'
foo(20,12) resumes: prints k=0 -> output: '2,0,'
foo(204,8) resumes: prints k=4 -> output: '2,0,4,'
foo(2048,0) resumes: prints k=8 -> output: '2,0,4,8,'
main resumes: printf("%d", sum) where sum=0 -> output: '2,0,4,8,0'
steps.note: sum in main is 0 because C passes integers by value; foo's local sum changes do not propagate back to main.

solution.result: The program prints: 8,4,0,2,0. Answer: B. (The digits are printed in the order from most-significant to least-significant as the stack unwinds, followed by main printing sum=0.)

questions.formula_note: Post-order recursive digit extraction: printing after the recursive call reverses the extraction order; pass-by-value means the caller's sum variable is unaffected.

questions.to_find: What set operation does the algorithm zzz compute?

understand.plain: Each set is stored as a boolean array where position i is 1 if element i is in the set. The algorithm computes z[i] = (x[i] AND NOT y[i]) OR (NOT x[i] AND y[i]) for each i. This is the exclusive OR (XOR) operation on each bit. In set theory, XOR of characteristic functions corresponds to the symmetric difference of sets.

keywords.explain: x[i]=1 means element i belongs to set X; x[i]=0 means it does not.
X delta Y = (X - Y) union (Y - X): elements in exactly one of the two sets, not in both.

understand.visual_alt: Truth table showing z[i] = x[i] XOR y[i]: z is 1 only when exactly one of x[i] or y[i] is 1, which corresponds to symmetric difference.

given.aim: Identify which set operation is implemented by the boolean expression z[i] = (x[i] AND NOT y[i]) OR (NOT x[i] AND y[i]).

terms.meaning: True when i is in X but not in Y — elements belonging to X - Y.
terms.connects: Captures the part of Z from set X that is not in Y.

terms.meaning: True when i is in Y but not in X — elements belonging to Y - X.
terms.connects: Captures the part of Z from set Y that is not in X.

terms.meaning: z[i] = 1 iff i is in exactly one of X or Y; this is logical XOR, equivalent to symmetric difference.
terms.example: x[i]=1, y[i]=1 -> (1 AND 0) OR (0 AND 1) = 0; element in both sets is excluded.
terms.connects: The union of the two exclusive parts equals the symmetric difference (X delta Y).

given.plan: Evaluate the boolean expression z[i] = (x[i] AND NOT y[i]) OR (NOT x[i] AND y[i]) for all four combinations of x[i] and y[i]. Recognize the truth table as XOR, which represents the symmetric difference (X-Y) union (Y-X).

steps.title: Evaluate the expression for all input cases
steps.formula_raw: z[i] = (x[i] AND NOT y[i]) OR (NOT x[i] AND y[i])
steps.apply:
Case x=0,y=0: (0 AND 1) OR (1 AND 0) = 0 OR 0 = 0 (i not in X or Y -> not in Z)
Case x=0,y=1: (0 AND 0) OR (1 AND 1) = 0 OR 1 = 1 (i in Y only -> in Z)
Case x=1,y=0: (1 AND 1) OR (0 AND 0) = 1 OR 0 = 1 (i in X only -> in Z)
Case x=1,y=1: (1 AND 0) OR (0 AND 1) = 0 OR 0 = 0 (i in both -> not in Z)
steps.note: This is exactly the XOR truth table: z[i] = x[i] XOR y[i].

steps.title: Identify the corresponding set operation
steps.formula_raw: Z = {i : i in X XOR i in Y} = (X - Y) union (Y - X)
steps.apply: z[i]=1 iff exactly one of x[i], y[i] is 1. In set terms, i is in Z iff i is in X but not Y, or in Y but not X. This is the symmetric difference: Z = (X - Y) union (Y - X).
steps.note: Symmetric difference is NOT union (which would include elements in both) and NOT intersection (which only includes elements in both).

solution.result: The set Z computed by algorithm zzz is (X - Y) union (Y - X), the symmetric difference of X and Y. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Boolean XOR as symmetric difference: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B) = A XOR B, which in set theory equals (A - B) union (B - A), the symmetric difference.
